questions.q: Savitch's theorem establishes a relationship between non-deterministic and deterministic space complexity. Which statement correctly captures this relationship?
options: NSPACE(f(n)) = DSPACE(f(n)), so non-determinism adds no power to space-bounded computation
NSPACE(f(n)) is a subset of DSPACE(f(n)^2), meaning deterministic simulation of NTMs requires squaring space
NSPACE(f(n)) requires exponential time but linear space, while DSPACE(f(n)) requires linear time
Deterministic Turing machines cannot simulate non-deterministic behavior with polynomial space overhead
questions.explanation: Savitch's theorem proves NSPACE(f(n)) ⊆ DSPACE(f(n)^2), meaning any non-deterministic f(n)-space computation can be simulated deterministically using O(f(n)^2) space. This differs from time complexity, where non-determinism may provide exponential speedup. Option 1 is false; option 3 confuses space and time; option 4 is contradicted by Savitch's result.

questions.q: The complexity class L (logarithmic space) is defined as DSPACE(log n). Which of the following statements is TRUE?
options: L = NL, meaning logarithmic space is deterministically equivalent to non-deterministic logarithmic space
L is a subset of P, because any algorithm in L runs in polynomial time
L contains only the empty language and finite languages
Every language in L requires at least one work tape in addition to the input tape
questions.explanation: Any L-algorithm uses O(log n) space, hence O(n^O(log n)) time (bounded by the number of configurations), which is polynomial time. Thus L ⊆ P. Option 1 is open; option 3 is false (L contains complex languages like PALINDROME). Option 4 confuses Turing machine models; standard definitions include work tapes.

questions.q: NL (non-deterministic logarithmic space) is relevant to understanding the role of non-determinism in space-bounded computation. Which language is known to be NL-complete?
options: SATISFIABILITY (SAT), the Boolean satisfiability problem
DIRECTED GRAPH REACHABILITY, deciding if there is a path from s to t in a directed graph
TRAVELING SALESMAN, finding a short tour visiting all vertices
HALTING PROBLEM, determining if a Turing machine halts on a given input
questions.explanation: DIRECTED REACHABILITY is known to be NL-complete and in NL (guess path edges one at a time, store only current vertex in O(log n) space). SAT is NP-complete. TSP is NP-hard. HALTING PROBLEM is undecidable. Option 1 correctly identifies a classic NL-complete problem representing the difficulty of path-finding with minimal space.

questions.q: PSPACE (polynomial space) is the union of DSPACE(n^k) over all constants k. How does PSPACE relate to NP and P?
options: NP is a subset of PSPACE, and P equals PSPACE
NP is a subset of PSPACE, but it is unknown whether P = PSPACE
PSPACE is a subset of NP, which explains why NP-hard problems are difficult
P and NP are incomparable to PSPACE; they are different measurement dimensions
questions.explanation: By Savitch's theorem, NP ⊆ NSPACE(poly) ⊆ DSPACE(poly^2) = PSPACE. Thus NP ⊆ PSPACE. Whether P = NP or P = PSPACE remains open. Option 1 assumes P = PSPACE, which is unproven. Option 2 correctly states the known relationships and open problems. Options 3 and 4 misstate the hierarchy.

questions.q: Space and time complexity are governed by different tradeoffs. Which statement about the space-time tradeoff is MOST accurate?
options: An algorithm can always reduce space by using more time, but this tradeoff has limits
Increasing time can never compensate for insufficient space in solving a problem
Space and time are independent; algorithms can optimize one without affecting the other
Time complexity is always more important than space complexity in practical computation
questions.explanation: Many algorithms demonstrate space-time tradeoffs: memoization uses more space to save time; streaming algorithms use less space but more time. However, fundamental limits apply—if a problem requires exponential time, no space reduction helps. Option 0 captures the nuance that tradeoffs exist but are bounded by problem hardness. Option 1 is too absolute; option 2 ignores tradeoffs; option 3 is subjective and false.

questions.q: The complexity class PSPACE-complete contains problems such as QUANTIFIED BOOLEAN FORMULA (QBF), where QBF is hardest in PSPACE. What distinguishes PSPACE-complete problems from NP-complete problems?
options: PSPACE-complete problems are easier because they use less memory
PSPACE-complete problems often require alternation between existential and universal quantification, exceeding the capabilities of a single NP oracle
PSPACE-complete problems are always solvable in polynomial time with exponential space
NP-complete problems are more fundamental than PSPACE-complete problems
questions.explanation: QBF with alternating quantifiers (∃∀∃...) cannot be reduced to SAT (which is purely existential) without exponential blowup. This alternation characterizes the AP hierarchy within PSPACE and distinguishes it from NP. Option 2 correctly identifies that alternation is the key difference. Option 0 is backwards; option 3 confuses solvability conditions; option 4 is opinion.

questions.q: A problem is in co-NL if its complement is in NL. Given that NL = co-NL (proven by Immerman and Szelepcsényi), what is the significance of this result?
options: Non-determinism in log-space computation is more balanced than in polynomial-time computation
The complement of any NL problem is also solvable in non-deterministic logarithmic space
Every NL-complete problem has a complement that is also NL-complete
Log-space computation is more powerful than polynomial-time computation
questions.explanation: NL = co-NL means the class is closed under complementation. This is remarkable because P = co-P is trivial (just negate the answer), but for NP, it is unknown whether NP = co-NP. The non-deterministic reachability problem and its complement (non-reachability) are both in NL. Option 1 uses 'balanced' vaguely; option 2 precisely states the result; option 3 is stronger than necessary; option 4 confuses complexity classes.

questions.q: In analyzing space-bounded computation, the linear space hierarchy theorem states that for space-constructible functions f and g with f(n) = o(g(n)), which relationship holds?
options: DSPACE(f(n)) = DSPACE(g(n)), space-bounded classes collapse at different constructible rates
DSPACE(f(n)) is a proper subset of DSPACE(g(n)), providing hierarchy of increasingly powerful space classes
DSPACE(f(n)) and DSPACE(g(n)) are incomparable if f and g are not polynomially related
Space hierarchy theorems do not exist; all space-bounded classes collapse to regular languages
questions.explanation: The space hierarchy theorem (for space-constructible f, g) proves DSPACE(f(n)) ⊂ DSPACE(g(n)) if f(n) = o(g(n)). This establishes a strict hierarchy: log(n) ⊂ log²(n) ⊂ ... ⊂ poly(n), unlike the time hierarchy which requires a log factor gap. Option 1 is false; option 2 correctly states the theorem; option 3 misunderstands constructibility; option 4 is false.

questions.q: A deterministic Turing machine with multiple work tapes can be simulated by a single-tape machine with what space overhead?
options: No overhead; single-tape and multi-tape machines have identical space complexity
O(n) overhead, because tape contents must be serialized sequentially
O(log n) overhead, using additional bits to track head positions
Polynomial overhead in the space bound itself, depending on the number of tapes
questions.explanation: A multi-tape TM can be simulated on a single tape by interleaving the tape contents and storing head positions as part of the state or in O(log n) auxiliary space (since positions are integers in [1, n]). The simulation does not require O(n) serialization overhead for space accounting. Option 0 is incorrect due to state representation; option 2 is precise; option 3 overshoots.

questions.q: When analyzing the space complexity of recursive algorithms, what is the relationship between recursion depth and space used on the call stack?
options: Recursion depth and stack space are independent; depth measures iterations while stack space measures memory
The call stack space is proportional to recursion depth times the space used per frame, which is critical in analyzing total space
Recursion depth has no bearing on space complexity because modern systems use dynamic memory allocation
Stack space is negligible compared to heap allocations in recursive algorithms
questions.explanation: Each recursive call creates a stack frame storing local variables, parameters, and return addresses. Total stack space = recursion depth × average frame size. For example, tree recursion with depth O(log n) and O(1) per frame uses O(log n) stack space. Option 1 confuses concepts; option 2 correctly identifies the relationship; option 3 ignores stack memory; option 4 oversimplifies.
